![Unsupervised algorithms
Clustering
Association rule mining
Principal component analysis
Kernel Density Estimation(KDE)
Gaussian Mixture of Model(GMM)
The set text for this module is:
﷟HYPERLINK "https://jakevdp.github.io/PythonDataScienceHandbook"Vanderplas, J. Python Data Science Handbook, O'Reilly
Clustering
Scatter plot with data organised into clusters, as described in the preceding text. 
Clustering can be useful for imputation. Missing values can be imputed based on values of others in the cluster. 
Could also assign members of a cluster a cluster ID, which can serve as a shorthand and save storage space and preserve privacy.
Centroid-based clustering
Density-based clustering
Distribution-based clustering
Hierarchical clustering
Centroid-based clustering
Centroid is middle point of cluster, usually arithmetic mean.
Assigned a cluster based on distance to a centroid. 
Non-hierarchical
Sensitive to initial conditions and outliers. 
K-means. K - cluster numbers is a parameter
Centroid-based clustering with four clusters as described in the preceding text.](data:image/png;base64,)  
![Centroid-based clustering with four clusters as described in the preceding text. 
Density-based clustering
Based on density of points in discrete areas.
Clusters can be of any shape. 
Outliers are not put into clusters.
Doesn't not handle differing densities and high dimensional data well.
Density-based clustering with two clusters as described in the preceding text. 
Distribution-based clustering
Clustering based on probabilistic distributions, like Gaussian distribution. 
Distribution-based clustering with three clusters as described in the preceding text. The centre of each cluster is highlighted.](data:image/png;base64,)  
![Distribution-based clustering with three clusters as described in the preceding text. The centre of each cluster is highlighted. 
Hierarchical clustering
Might be good for say biology.
Hierarchical clustering with clusters within clusters as described in the preceding text. Points are lettered A through to G. The relationship between the points is described in the Dendrogram on the right hand-side.  
Prepare data. Normalisation, scaling, handling missing values, and check that similarity can be meaningfully assessed.
Create similarity metric. 
Run clustering algorithm
Interpret results and make adjustments. No ground truth to compare against.
Normalisation is moving values in a range like 0 to 1. (x-min)/(max-min)
Scaling - sets mean to 0, with std=1
(X-mean)/std
K-means
Treats data as roughly circular distributions around a centroid.
Trail and error to work out best value of k. 
Assumes all values are numeric. Or non-numeric attributes need to be mapped to numeric values.
K points are randomly selected
Points are assigned to nearest centroid.
New centroids are calculated within each cluster by finding the mean value
Repeat above until centroids are stabilised.](data:image/png;base64,)  
![K-means++ algorithm
First centroid is randomly selected.
Other centroids are set by selecting ones furthest from previously chosen centroid.
Same as above.
Since it's non-deterministic, you might want to run it a few times.
To find the best value of k
Elbow method
Silhouette score
Elbow method. Calculate within-cluster sum of squares(WCSS)
Graph showing the k-value (x-axis) against the WCSS (y-axis) as described in the preceding text. 
However there might not be a sharp curve
Elbow graph with no clear elbow, as described in the preceding text.](data:image/png;base64,)  
![Elbow graph with no clear elbow, as described in the preceding text. 
Silhouette score
Ranges from -1 to 1.
Points are perfectly assigned ina cluster and clusters are easily distinguishable
Clusters are overlapping
-1. Points are wrongly assigned in a cluster
Silhouette score = (b-a)/max(a,b)
a=average intra-cluster distance
B=average inter-cluster distance
The parameters of the silhouette score, as described in the preceding text. 
Similarity metric
With a single feature, similarity is the Euclidean distance between plotted data points
For several features
Normalise and or scale data
Calculate similarity of each feature to each centroid
Calculate average similarity across features for each data point for each centroid
It's difficult for categorical values, since the mapped numbers don't have any meaning. If mapping into a space that captures semantics, that might be useful. 
Post codes could be concerted to longitude and latitude
Colours could be represented by RGB](data:image/png;base64,)  
![Colours could be represented by RGB
Distance could use Jaccard similarity. Dividing intersectional cardinality(number of features values in common) of two sets by union cardinality(total number of features in the combined sets). 
Similarity = 1/(1+distance)
@TODO
Sklearn has a number of different clustering algorithms available in its sklearn.cluster module (see ﷟HYPERLINK "https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/clustering.html"2.3. Clustering — scikit-learn 1.5.2 documentation).
Note the table in 2.3.1 of the referenced site which gives the method name, parameters, scalability, use case, and similarity metric used. Algorithms relating to the methods discussed in the article are:
K-means – for centroid based clustering. 
DBSCAN – for density-based clustering. 
Gaussian mixtures – for probability-based clustering. 
agglomerative clustering – for hierarchical clustering.
Look at the sklearn article at ﷟HYPERLINK "https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/clustering.html"2.3. Clustering — scikit-learn 1.5.2 documentationwhich provides a Python example programme demonstrating a number of sklearn’s clustering methods.
Implement and run the code. 
Check that you understand the code. Make a note of any part of which you are unsure. 
What sort of output do you get? 
Why do the different algorithms give different results? 
What is anisotropicly distributed data? 
What type of clustering is the Birch method? 
What type of clustering is the Ward method?
K mini batch takes samples so converges faster, but end result isn't as good.
Affinity Propagation Messages between pairs are sent till they converge. It's slow
Mean shift centroid based
Spectral clustering low dimensional embedding of the affinity matrix
Heirarchical clustering nested clusters.
Ward minimises the sum of squared differences within all clusters. A variance minimizing approach.
BIRCH Clustering Feature Tree(CFT). Kind of lossy compression to Clustering Features Nodes.](data:image/png;base64,)  
![The CF Subclusters hold the necessary information for clustering which prevents the need to hold the entire input data in memory. This information includes:
Number of samples in a subcluster.
Linear Sum - An n-dimensional vector holding the sum of all samples
Squared Sum - Sum of the squared L2 norm of all samples.
Centroids - To avoid recalculation linear sum / n_samples.
Squared norm of the centroids.
The BIRCH algorithm has two parameters, the threshold and the branching factor. The branching factor limits the number of subclusters in a node and the threshold limits the distance between the entering sample and the existing subclusters.
Association rule mining
Retail
Cross selling
Market basket analysis
Cross selling suggesting to customers who buy x that they might also like y.
Market basket analysis what is brought together.
Did a marketing campaign work, has customers buying habits changed, does the location of products affect sales.
IF antecedent THEN consequent
Support: ratio of transactions that contains the antecedent and consequent to the total number of transactions
Confidence: The ratio of transactions that contain both antecedent and consequent to the transactions that just contain antecedent.
Lift: A high lift indicates a strong association
Conviction: Whether rule is there by chance or not
Leverage: Difference between observation and if they were independent.
Untitled picture.png](data:image/png;base64,)  
![Untitled picture.png 
Usually prune rules to those with high support and high confidence. 
Demographic data can make it more powerful.
E.g. 
If male and <35 then if hotdogs THEN beer
Algorithms
Apriori
Eclat
GP-Growth(Frequent Pattern Growth)
Apriori algorithm
Find all combinations of items in a set of transactions which occur with a min freq. Frequent item sets.
Generate rules that express the probable co-occurrence within frequent item sets.
Install efficient_apriori by using the following command at the command prompt:
Pip install efficient_apriori
There is also an Apriori algorithm in the mlextend.frequent-patternslibrary:
from mlxtend.frequent_patterns import apriori
Take a look at ﷟HYPERLINK "https://pypi.org/project/efficient-apriori/"efficient-apriori · PyPI and try out the example given on the page.
What new coding constructs do you see?
Try to implement the worked example above using similar code. You will need to transform the data to a similar style of that shown in the website example.](data:image/png;base64,)  
![Try to implement the worked example above using similar code. You will need to transform the data to a similar style of that shown in the website example.
It's simple and robust. But is compute intensive, requiring multiple scans of the dataset.
You can read more about Apriori at BotPenguin (n.d.):
BotPenguin (n.d.) ‘Apriori Algorithm’, BotPenguin, Available at: ﷟HYPERLINK "https://botpenguin.com/glossary/apriori-algorithm"Apriori Algorithm: Advantages & Disadvantages | BotPenguin (accessed 15-10-24)
FP-Growth has better performance. Creates a header table only of items that meet support, scanned and FP Tree is created.
Mudumba, B. and Kabir, M.F., 2024. ‘Mine-first association rule mining: An integration of independent frequent patterns in distributed environments’, Decision Analytics Journal, 10, p.100434. Available at: ﷟HYPERLINK "https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2772662224000389"Mine-first association rule mining: An integration of independent frequent patterns in distributed environments - ScienceDirect (accessed 15-10-24)
ECLAT(Equivalence Class Clustering and Bottom-Up Lattice Traversal)
Depth-first search-based approach to find frequent item sets. It uses the notion of "equivalence class" to reduce search space.
Read more about Eclat at ﷟HYPERLINK "https://quality-life.medium.com/eclat-algorithm-in-machine-learning-fe07d33fcc5b"Eclat Algorithm in Machine Learning | by BetterLife | Medium
Principal component analysis
The curse of dimensionality, relates to the number of features in a dataset.
High dimensions means
Hard to get reliable results
Getting a representative sample is difficult.
Computationally expensive to run.
PCA is the main method to reduce dimensions. Used in preparation and exploration phases.
Designed to reduce dimensions while keeping the maximum variance of data.
It can come at the cost of accuracy, but could also help deal with overfitting and noise.
Easier to explore and visualise datasets. Make ML algorithms faster.
Principal components are linear combinations of initial variables. The new variables are uncorrelated.
Principal components hold most information about variance between the features. 
An ordered list of principal components is made based on the information each holds about the variance between features.](data:image/png;base64,)  
![An ordered list of principal components is made based on the information each holds about the variance between features. 
Need to select what coverage you want, 90%/95% etc.
Bar chart showing principal components 1-6 with their percentage of explained variance: 1: 60%, 2: 20%, 3: 10%, 4: 5%, 5: 2%, and 6: 2%. 
PCA, first component is line of best fit - that accounts for most variance.
Second is a line that orthogonal to line of best fit which accounts for next highest variance.
Continue up to the number of features/dimensions
Variance of each dimension is given by calculating a covariance matrix. Then for each colum of the matrix an eigenvector is obtained and eigenvalue calculated.
Eigenvalue is a eigenvector and eigenvalue is the variance.
Covariance(i, j) = average of [(feature_i − mean_i) * (feature_j − mean_j)]
Principal component analysis in five dimensions as described in the preceding text. 
Steps](data:image/png;base64,)  
![Steps
Standardisation
Covariance matrix
Calculate eigenvectors and eigenvalues
Feature matrix
Recast data
Standardisation. 
Z=(value-mean)/sd
Covariance matrix
A n*n symmetric matrix with n the number of dimensions.
Covariance is a measure of how much on average datapoints varies from the mean.
Untitled picture.png 
Untitled picture.png 
The sign indicates the the direction of the relationship.
0 indicates no relationship
The eigenvector of electronics is [0.63, 0.34, -0.25]
Eigen value is AX=𝜆X
Ranking the eigenvectors in order of eigenvalues is the PCA order of significance. Percent of variance is eigenvalue/total eigenvalues
Feature matrix
Then decide what you are keeping, and make a feature matrix just of them.](data:image/png;base64,)  
![Then decide what you are keeping, and make a feature matrix just of them. 
is n × k, where:
n = original number of features
k = number of components you keep
Recast the data
FinalDataSet = FeatureVectorT * StandardisedOriginalDataSetT
Sklearn has a class called PCA in its sklearn.decomposition library.
One of the parameters you specify when using the method is the number of principal components needed.
You can also carry out PCA by using methods direct from Python’s linear algebra library.
Bibliography
Apiletti, D., Baralis, E., Cerquitelli, T., Garza, P., Pulvirenti, F. and Venturini, L.(2017) ‘Frequent itemsets mining for big data: a comparative analysis’, Big data research, 9, pp.67-83.
